speaker: interviewer
text: Roll three, Jack Voorzanger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Voorz--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Voorzanger, I want to ask you another question about that time before the war ended. And what I wanted to ask was whether or not you had any idea or heard anything about where your parents might be or what was happening to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely not. We were kept completely in the dark. Nobody spoke about it. The only thing that they said, you know, that the Jews were being deported. But nobody said anything about where, what, why, how. And the village I lived in-- there was a population of 4,500 people. 100% of them was all Roman Catholic. And their sentiment against Jews was very strong. Just like I said, we usually-- you know, because crucified Christ, like what Mr. De Cock used to tell me, you know, what they said.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it has been through the centuries that when they crucified him, Pontius Pilate said, you want this man to die? Or I don't know, something like that. And the people they said, his blood shall be over us and our children. And Mr. De Cock told me this was the curse we bestowed upon ourselves and we are being punished for that and will we be punished for that throughout the centuries till the judgment day. So that was the feeling of  a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nobody talked about anything. There was nothing in the newspapers. We didn't have a newspaper. We didn't have a radio until almost to the end of the war. All we did was eat, work, sleep, and go to church.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel any lingering feeling about being Jewish? Did you-- you know, did you still feel some--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --consciousness about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not, because I was educated-- we didn't have a Jewish home, per se, at home. And when I was being educated as a Catholic that overtook everything that I ever had. That's why I became such a devout Catholic. And all I did was pray for my parents to come back. So I had no feeling about my own Jewish-ness at all. That all disappeared when I became a Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it your intention also to become a priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just wanted to please the man. Anything he wanted me to do, I did. I just wanted to please him, just because of-- to prevent being hit. He hit me for the craziest things. There was a man who was in that preventorium who had a violin. And I was musically inclined, and I loved the music. My father was a music lover. So he gave me his violin. And he said, you know, that I should learn to play the violin. It was a little cheap violin, you know. So I was trying to play a tune. I could play a tune on one string.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day I came home, and I went to the violin, and I saw there was a crack in it. And I got very upset. So I went to him because I knew one of the kids had done it. And I went to him. I said, look, there is a crack in the violin. He got so mad at me. He grabbed the violin, and he hit it over my head in a million pieces. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] So I did everything possible to please the man because I was scared stiff. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I had bad grades, in math especially. I was very bad in math, failing grades all the time. So during the summer vacation, he made me study. And he gave me problems in the morning before he went to work. And he expected me to have them finished by the time he came home. And there was no way I could figure it out. Because there was nobody who could explain it to me. There was no teacher. And I couldn't do it. So when he came home and I didn't do it, then he would hit me with the book. And then the next morning he did it over and over again. And if I didn't finish it the next day, then he hit me with the book, and he sent to bed without dinner. So being a priest was just a good thing for me to be out of the house, number one, away from him, and also to please him. Now after the war ended, you were in contact with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my brother came back from Germany-- as I told you-- he got a job in Antwerp, in the bank. And they discovered, in the physical, that he had developed tuberculosis. So he was being treated in a sanatorium near Dinant. This is where the famous Battle of the Bulge was fought around there, beautiful countryside. And he was there for over a year, I think. In the meantime, I was in a seminary studying to be a priest. And we were allowed to write one letter a week to either parents, or relatives, or whatever. So I had to write to him, Mr. De Cock. So I couldn't write to my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: To make things worse, the sanatorium called Maria De Cock and Mr. De Cock to come immediately. And we thought there was something wrong with my brother. So he took a train and we went up there. And we found out that he had seduced a nun, and she had given up her vows for him and left the abbey-- is that what they call it? So, of course, they dismissed him right away. Punishment for me was that I should never see my brother again. Had you been very close with your brother till then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love him. [CRYING] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother got back from the sanatorium, and he went back to the bank business in Antwerp. And he-- What year was this, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] '46, yeah, 1946. And he became a banker in the bank in Antwerp. And he rented a little place with room and board in Antwerp. I was not allowed to write to him because of what happened. And after I was dismissed from the school-- the seminary I was going to-- I was not allowed to see him. Of course, he never came to see me either, because there was bad blood between Maria De Cock and my brother, of course because of what happened in the sanatorium. So I lost all contact with him until 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I loved him very much. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] So when my brother-- he went to Holland and he tried to find if there were anymore relatives alive from the Voorzanger family. And he found that my father's brother was alive still. And my aunt--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob. He was my father's youngest brother, and my aunt, Aunt Emy. And he had a, a fur store. He would make fur coats, custom made, your well-to-do. And my brother and him, they became-- they made a close relationship. Meantime, I was still in Westmalle. I didn't even know this was going on. My brother never told me that my uncle was still alive because he was not allowed to see me. I was not allowed to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my uncle convinced my brother that he should come and live with him. He said, I have a big house here. Why don't you come and move in, live here, you know? You are intelligent man. You have no problem getting a job here in a bank. Why do you have to live in such a crummy place, in that little apartment with room and board? You know, you'll have a beautiful house. So he convinced my brother that my brother was going to make the move and move back to Holland to live with my uncle. How was your uncle-- how did your uncle survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt was German. She was not Jewish. I never found out what happened during the war years but somehow she was managing to keep him out of the concentration camp somehow. I don't know how. I never-- I don't  know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my brother decided that he was going to move to Holland, he went to Mr. De Cock who worked in a newspaper in Antwerp. He was personnel manager or something there. I don't know. And he told him that he was going to move to Holland, and he wanted to see me to say goodbye to me before he left. So when Mr. De Cock told me that, he said, I'll tell you what. He said, you know, you can-- your brother is leaving Monday. He said, you can see him Saturday. You can go and visit him, say goodbye to him, under one condition-- I want the yard cleaned up. I want the front lawn cleaned up. I want the whole house being scrubbed, the floors. And I want all the shoes polished. And he said, you know, then you can go and see him. So of course, I worked to get this all done. And by-- I think it was about one o'clock or 12 o'clock, noon-- I was finished. And he came to inspect everything. And he said, OK. So he gave me some money to go to the-- get the streetcar to Antwerp. And I went to see my brother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I hadn't seen him for four years. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So he asked me, he said, Jack, what are you going to do with your life? You have no profession. You have no education except the time that you spent in seminary. You have no job. You have a miserable life with these people. What do you want to do? Said, I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] So I said to him, I said, I would like to go with you. He said, well, he says come on. He said, let's go and call Uncle Jacob in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I'll ask him if it is OK if I take you too. So we called from the Grand Central Station in Antwerp. And he said, by all means, absolutely. You bring him with you. I was so happy. But now the difficult part came. I had to go down back to Westmalle and tell him that I was going to go to live with my uncle. He was in the backyard when I came home there. And she was in the living room. And when I came in the house, she said, well, did you say goodbye to the pig? I said, no. She said, No? I said, no. I said, I want to go with him to Holland. She said, oh. And she ran out and she told her husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came running in. And he yelled at me. And he screamed at me-- everything I have done for you. And in very dramatic, he looked at me, and he said that the Iron Curtain has fallen. I want you out of the house immediately. I said, OK. And I went upstairs to get my stuff. He said, you don't take nothing with you. He said, you came with nothing. You're going to leave with nothing. I wanted to call my brother, but my brother had a girlfriend in Brussels. He wasn't home. So I left Sunday morning at 4 o'clock. I went to Antwerp and went to the Dutch consulate to get a passport. I went to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just go back, just a little bit. I wanted to ask you-- at that time when you met your brother for the first time in so many years, were you able to confide in him about what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why he-- yeah, I told him what the things which have happened to me. And that's why he told me at the time, what are you going to do with your life. You know, you are unhappy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by that point, had you already come to terms with, with the possibility that you might not be seeing your parents again? Did you know already what had happened or where they might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I still didn't realize that until I came to live with my uncle. Because my uncle was recalling stories, what happened between him and my father, you know, childhood stories, teenage stories, things which I had never known. And that's when I realized [SOBBING] that was it, never see them again. And that was actually the first time in seven years-- from 1942 to 1949-- that I realized how much I missed them and how much I had missed during those seven years of growing up as a teenager without parents. That's when I realized it. I was 19 years old already. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] [SIGHS] It's like it happened yesterday. [PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS] So my whole life changed in 1949. I lived with people who spoke my language, Dutch, which I hadn't spoken in seven years because we spoke Flemish. And I got a job right away in the camera store, in the darkroom making prints, black and white. But then [LAUGHS] the story of my life again, my uncle-- I was making 25 guilders a week. And, my uncle said that I should pay him 20 guilders a week for room and board. And he insisted that I should buy, every Friday, flowers for my aunt because she loved flowers. And for the rest of the money, I could buy cigarettes and buy clothing. 25 guilders was not-- or five guilders pocket money, after you had to buy flowers. Flowers were about a guilder. So that I had no money to buy clothing. My brother got fed up living with my uncle. He had a girlfriend in Brussels. So in December of 1949 my brother decided he was going back to Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here again, [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I was alone. My uncle [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] was a strange man in the fact that my aunt complained constantly about how he was behaving when he was taking measurements of the women's coats, things like that, plus the money problems that I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: On top of that they had hired a maid, beautiful young girl, my age. And I fell in love with her. And my uncle didn't want me to date her. And he never told me why. But I did it anyhow. We went in secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all this combination of the money, and the fights that he had with his wife, and the fact that I had to pay him so much, and I had no money of myself, and the fact that I was loving this girl and he didn't want me to. You know, I was-- I had to talk to somebody about it, and the only one was my brother. So one night they went to a movie. And I grabbed a typewriter and I wrote my brother a long letter complaining about what life was like with my uncle. I sealed the envelope and put my brother's name on the envelope, Mr. J. Voorzanger, and his address in Belgium. I didn't have a  stamp. So I put the envelope in my pocket, in my jacket, and I went to bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the night there was came a huge rainstorm and my uncle came into my room to close the window of my room and my jacket fell off the hook. And he saw the envelope that I had written to my brother. And he opened it. And he read the letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next morning when I got out of bed he said, I want you out of the house immediately. So I asked him, is it-- how dare you to open this letter. He said, well, I thought it was addressed to me, J. Voorzanger. I said, yeah, but you don't live in Antwerp, don't you? So I said, fine. So, I went to work. The girl I was in love with, she said to me, you can live with my parents. We have two floors that you can sleep there. So I went to sleep there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to the Ministry of War, and I asked them if I could be drafted sooner. And in October 1950, they drafted me into the Dutch Air Force.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question for you about that time. And I wanted to ask you whether you were still as devout a Catholic during that period after you left the De Cock home.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The minute I left Westmalle when I went to my brother on my way to Holland, my brother as-- that was  on a Sunday morning. And as a devoted Catholic, you have to go to church every Sunday. So my brother said to me, he said, you know, you can go to church if you want to. He said, it won't bother me. I said, no, I don't want to go no more. And I never set foot in a church again after that. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you feel yourself Jewish then at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that came later. That came much later. But I was always proud to tell everybody that I was Jewish. Because when I went into the Air Force and they had my statistics, religion-- it said Jewish. So I never hid it from anybody that I was Jewish. I did not-- how do you say-- pursue the religion, but I felt myself a Jew. I wanted to be a Jew, because my father was a Jew. My mother was a Jew. And there was nothing to be ashamed about. No matter what they told me, it [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now were you required to go into the armed forces in Holland at that time? Yeah, I was drafted, yeah. In October 1950 I was drafted in the Air Force. And I did boot camp for three months. And then after three months they transferred me into photo department. And there was a-- I started to be an assistant photographer. And this is where I learned most of my trade because they had the best equipment in the world. No expense was too much. And I was in the Air Force for almost two years. And that's when I really learned my trade. During that time did you have contact with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Now my brother and I, we had a fall out. We discovered that my father had a life insurance. Well, my brother had discovered that. And it was very small. And we were going to get the money, and it was going to be split between us 50-50. But my brother was getting married while I was in the Air Force. And he want to know if I would give him my half. And I said, sure, you can have  my half. But it took so long for them to pay out the money that by the time they paid I was discharged from the Air Force. And I had absolutely no money. I had no clothing. So I used the money to buy clothing for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Voorzanger, we're going to end this tape.
